blurb: The North Cobb Senior Center is closing out the holiday week with a patriotic celebration on Friday morning: a presentation on the history of the American flag, a formal Color Guard ceremony, live patriotic music from Debra Lieb, and red, white, and blue treats after. It's a low-key, meaningful way to mark the 250th anniversary in a format that's a little more personal than a big crowd event. If you're looking for something that honors the week without requiring an evening out, this one fits. Free to attend, runs from 10:30 to 11:30 AM.
scoring_notes: Fits the older segment of East Cobb's empty nester audience well. Patriotic theme is perfectly timed with the week. Acworth is a bit of a stretch geographically for core East Cobb readers, and the senior center setting narrows the appeal. Still a legitimate community event worth including.

blurb: If you've thought about cutting your water use and doing something practical for your yard at the same time, this is the hands-on version of that conversation. Cobb County is hosting a Rain Barrel Make and Take on Thursday at noon where you actually build a rainwater harvesting barrel connected to a home downspout. You leave with something functional, not just a pamphlet. It's an hour, it's free, and for homeowners who care about their landscaping and their utility bills, it's genuinely useful. East Cobb has no shortage of people in that category.
scoring_notes: High homeownership rate in East Cobb makes a practical home improvement event relevant. The hands-on format gives it more value than a standard informational session. Free. Scores lower on uniqueness since it's a recurring county program format, but the practical takeaway (an actual rain barrel) differentiates it.

event_name: Escape to the Past: History Book Club
blurb: If you've been meaning to read Jonathan Alter's biography of Jimmy Carter and just haven't gotten around to it, this is the nudge. The history book club at Switzer Library is meeting Friday morning to discuss His Very Best: Jimmy Carter, A Life, and the timing is pretty perfect given everything that's happened over the last year. Carter was a complicated figure and Alter's take on him is thorough. The conversation around a biography like this tends to go places a standard book club doesn't. It's free, it's an hour, and it's the kind of Friday morning that's worth rearranging your schedule for if you're retired or work from home.
scoring_notes: Well-suited for East Cobb's educated, older-skewing empty nester segment. Jimmy Carter biography discussion has topical relevance. Free and low-commitment. Scores lower on uniqueness since book clubs are recurring, but the specific subject matter and timing give it real appeal for the right reader.

event_name: CCPL Celebrates America 250 with Marietta History Center: Revolutionary America
blurb: Mountain View Regional Library is partnering with the Marietta History Center for a hands-on American history program on Thursday afternoon, right in the middle of the America 250 anniversary year. It's geared toward younger kids but works well for families who want to keep the Fourth of July energy going a little longer into the week. Think crafts, activities, and actual context behind the Revolution, not just dates and names. It's an hour, it's free, and it's a few miles from most of East Cobb. If you've got kids home for the summer looking for something to do on a Thursday afternoon, this is a solid answer.
scoring_notes: Fits the family segment of East Cobb's audience, especially those with younger teens or kids home for summer. Timed well with the America 250 moment. Free and local. Lower uniqueness score because library programming runs regularly, but the America 250 tie-in and Marietta History Center partnership give it some distinction.

event_name: Dynamic Downsizing: Renovating for Maximum Return
venue: Tim D. Lee Senior Center, Marietta
blurb: If the idea of selling your home in the next few years has crossed your mind, this is worth an hour and a half of your Thursday night. The Janice Overbeck Real Estate Team is running a free seminar on how to sort through what you've accumulated, decide what stays and what goes, and organize your home in a way that actually positions you for a strong sale. It's practical, it's local, and it's presented by people who specialize in exactly this market. East Cobb homes carry real value, and how you prepare matters. This is part of a four-part series, so if it resonates, there's more where it came from. No cost to attend, just show up at 7 PM on Thursday.
scoring_notes: With a median age of 42 and a strong empty nester segment, downsizing content is highly relevant. High homeownership rate (78%) means many readers have equity they care about protecting. Free seminar with practical, financially relevant content for a high-income, educated audience.

event_name: The Georgia Room Presents: Revolutionary Soldiers of Marietta National Cemetery
blurb: Coming off the Fourth of July weekend, this one hits differently. Marietta National Cemetery historian Brad Quinlin is giving a free presentation at Switzer Library on Monday evening about two American Revolutionary War veterans actually buried right here in Marietta. Their names are John Hames and John Clark, and the story of how soldiers from the Revolution ended up laid to rest in Cobb County is genuinely fascinating. It's an hour of your evening, it's free, and it's the kind of local history most people drive past every day without ever knowing. If you've got a teen who just did a history unit on the Revolution, bring them. If you're the person who always says you want to learn more about the area you live in, here's your chance. Monday, 6 PM, no ticket needed.
scoring_notes: East Cobb skews educated and curious. A specialized local history presentation timed right after Independence Day is a strong fit. Free, local, and genuinely unique content about Marietta's own Revolutionary War connection. Works well for the established family and empty nester crowd.

event_name: Atlanta Braves vs. New York Mets (4th of July Night Game)
blurb: There is no better way to spend the Fourth of July than watching the Braves take on the Mets under the fireworks at Truist Park. First pitch is 8:08 PM, which means the game runs right into prime fireworks territory and the Battery will be electric all night. This is the kind of July 4th plan that writes itself: grab the family, get there early, find your seats, and let the evening take care of itself. The Mets rivalry has some real heat to it, so expect a crowd that's into it from the first inning. Tickets are selling for this one, and July 4th at Truist always draws a full house. Don't wait until Saturday afternoon to figure it out.
scoring_notes: Braves baseball on the Fourth of July is a perfect fit for East Cobb's established family and empty nester mix. Truist Park is basically in their backyard. The holiday date and Mets rivalry elevate this above a routine game night. High income audience handles variable ticket pricing without issue.
